text: that we can build those decision support tools for folks, I think has been the unlock for us. Yeah. I'm going to stay in the front. Yeah. I'd like to ask you, Ben, you said use numeric for some EVA analysis. So do you have some examples of how a tool has been able to, like, use AI to do better analysis, maybe better than, like, Martin, you were saying, like, churn that gives you days in the month, like really looking at maybe hiring happens sooner or faster later, or it was just timing from an event or trade show. So any examples of how it really works well for you? Yeah, happy to. So I think it sounds like you've also built this internally, but essentially what it will be is when we do our close and we're doing our flux analysis, Numeric has an integration with Ironclad, with NetSuite, and so it's able to use both of those contexts to understand when we see variance to plan or variance month-on-month, you could double-click into a specific GL and understand what are the drivers of that GL change. Is it that a certain vendor is now being billed that wasn't previously? Is it that your usage of a given vendor has now meaningfully shifted? And so that what I think helps us just really tighten in on is when you see BVA, it's like, OK, how much of this is true favorability or unfavorability or how much of this is timing? You can just get to that answer much faster. And so you can understand, OK, this is what I actually think is part of whether it's a flash or something. The explanation to the finance team and or me around, OK, this is actually what we think is going on underneath the hood. I think when it comes to your point about more rote work of finance, I think our flash reporting is becoming very much automated because it's something that follows a pretty similar format month to month. I mean, I don't know if you would radically change your flash every month. That would be kind of crazy. So AI is really good at that. It's like you have a pretty deterministic kind of format with information that's coming to it, and it actually is able to do a pretty good first pass at like, here's the flash. and then a human in the loop can then go and correct wording and correct understandings of what's driving it. Yeah, I think a high-level flux it can do, super detailed, real insights, not really. Yeah, and then just kind of one other story. I know just kind of talking with peers in the industry, kind of one approach I've seen is kind of get your budget data get your GL data get the procurement tool data into like one snowflake kind of database and then kind of unleash AI there But then I also seen like good use cases where you can go into all these tools that aren necessarily triangulate So basically like the budget information, trial balance information, and like some tools, they can kind of just go deep flux analysis just on NetSuite data. but if, oh, you have a big increase in marketing spend, if that was already a campaign in the budget, that's not as useful of information. So I think kind of that orchestration layer and kind of that single pane of glass across everything, no matter how, depending on how you structure your data to get there, is where I've seen some good results. You've got to really love paying snowflake invoices, though, to put all your data from all your different sources in there. You're paying it somewhere. Yeah. Yeah, we'll see. In pain or violence. You don't pay for like a NetSuite query, right? Whereas you do pay for a Snowflake query. Yeah, we'll see. We'll see. Yeah. Yeah. Hi, Johnny from Blue Light. Question, have either any three of you incorporated AI as a decision tree when evaluating headcount requests as part of your intake or that field process? sense? Short answer. No, not yet. Any ideas? No, I think very much I would say we haven't sort of relied on AI to... What we have done as part of that, because we've also done the hiring dynamic of like, okay, think through the interview loops, how do you make sure you're hiring people who are more AI oriented? One thing we've used AI as part of the hiring process, less about whether or not from a headcount, forecast or not perspective, is actually on the interview loop side whereby you obviously have you know let's call it half a dozen people plus who will interview a candidate they all have their scorecards they all talk about like the pros and cons of this candidate in their specific areas of domain what we are doing which i think is a little um what's the word i want to use it's a little archaic but our ceo wants to do it and our cpo is accommodating to it and i'm also so which is like my our ceo and myself will actually sign off on every hire we make We also not hiring crazy amounts of people so it is possible but I wouldn necessarily say recommend it necessarily But what is helpful is we using AI to basically go through all of the scorecard data of an interview candidate and then surface up to myself or our CEO like here are the areas of strength of this candidate Here are the areas of risks that were identified as part of the interview loop. These are some considerations to have. And then at least that enables us to have a faster look into a given candidate to understand, like, OK, what are the areas that we maybe want to actually go in and read the actual notes on a specific area of strength or risk versus the pages upon pages of interview notes, which also I will say, we're obviously using AI tools as part of the interview loop as it relates to just taking notes as you're interviewing candidates. And so that's really helpful because you're just getting more robust, more rich note taking for the reviewer of those notes, but also for the candidate experience is better because you're not having someone that's half paying attention because they're taking notes. They're actually able to engage more fluently with the candidate. Basically, they're all the same thing. So our CEO approves every single hire and we do hire a lot of people. So that's sort of somewhat of a burden. Then we use BrightHire in all the interviews to take notes. It's actually, not to plug BrightHire, but it also gives you feedback on yourself. Like how long is your longest monologue? What was the candidates to your speaking ratio? How many words per minute? How do you score? I'm like in the high 80s. But now that I sort of know the criteria, I was like, you get bonus points if you set an agenda at the beginning of the interview or something I've never done before. But now to make sure I get a good score, I'm like, today we're going to talk about this. Which apparently the candidates also like. We're learning. And shorter monologues. But yeah, so we used Brad Hire for that. And then we built our own little tool to take all the scorecards and sort of come up with a summary for our CEO. and then he says no and then we got to fight a little bit and then we get the hire approved. I did see this actually on my report from the HR team written in V0 later today. 100% offer acceptance. Wow. Congrats. But that was not without a fight. There's always a little... And at risk of saying this in front of a group of maybe people, maybe that means your offers are too high. You don ask them if they going to accept before you offer it right No OK Good stuff So just a couple of minutes left I love to each of you kind of give one wisdom looking back all the success you had on kind of reaching kind of AI at scale within your finance stack and operations, kind of room of finance and HR professionals, kind of one piece of advice. I would say just start. Like, just use it and try to find the time in your busy schedules to use it and get more familiar with it. And not, to your point, Martin, earlier, don't be fearful of it. Don't fear of failure. Don't fear of using it. I think that I actually was relatively slow to adopt AI at work because I was just like, I've got a million things to do. I've got back-to-back meetings. I've got some deliverable I need to ship next week. And I was just like, where am I going to find the time to go and jam for three, four hours on this tool? and so I think I was very slow to adopt in a way that I probably should have been faster and I would say had I done it all again I'm much quicker off the bat. Yes, I think it's a cultural thing. So lead by example, you can even say, hey, I had an hour on the weekend. I built this thing. It's probably terrible. I bet some of you that deal with this problem every single day and can commit a little bit more time to it can probably figure out to build a much better and more useful application than I did. But I'm experimenting. I'm trying it out. I don't think it's too crazy to say that AI is the future. So yeah, just have fun, honestly. It's easier to be afraid of it because there's lots of new words and complexity. But it just comes down to, it's almost easier than Microsoft Excel because you don't even need to do any formulas. You can just write plain English. And then you can say, you did it wrong. Do this instead. And then it just does it. so you need some patience maybe but yeah experiment and have some fun and learn and then it doesn't work and if you're asked to do the exact same thing again because somehow the machine started doing something completely different you ask it to change the location of a box and it rewrote the entire application instead sometimes weird stuff happens so be patient and have some fun great thanks Martin, Ben, thank you thank you all Thank you.
